channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Divisive F.D.A. Vaccine Regulator Is Resigning
item.description: Dr. Vinay Prasad drew criticism for overriding career scientists and rejecting drugs by companies seeking agency approval.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:description: Dr. Vinay Prasad’s refusal to accept Moderna’s application for a new mRNA flu vaccine, prompting an uproar among companies and experts.

item.title: Iran Attacks and Mideast Flights: How Safe Is Air Travel?
item.description: Drone and missile attacks have caused high anxiety across the region, but experts say the danger to commercial airliners is “fairly remote.”
item.dc:creator: Gabe Castro-Root
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An Emirates airliner evacuating foreigners from Dubai arrived in Frankfurt on Tuesday.

item.title: What to know about the report.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember

item.title: From 2001: Beate Uhse, 81, Entrepreneur in the Business of Erotic Goods
item.description: She developed Europe’s biggest emporium of erotic goods and opened a three-story sex museum in Germany.
item.dc:creator: Wolfgang Saxon
item.media:description: Beate Uhse in 1971, in front of her mail-order company headquarters in Flensburg, Germany.

item.title: From 1973: Mrs. Marjorie Merriweather Post Is Dead at 86
item.description: The owner of General Foods Corporation, she was a generous philanthropist whose great wealth also allowed her to entertain lavishly and live regally.
item.dc:creator: Special to The New York Times
item.media:description: Marjorie Merriweather Post in 1957 at her home in Washington.

item.title: From 2004: Estée Lauder, Pursuer of Beauty and Cosmetics Titan, Dies at 97
item.description: Her company, known for its creams, powders, ointments and perfumes, was estimated to be worth about $5 billion when it went public in 1995.
item.dc:creator: Richard Severo
item.media:description: Estée Lauder at her office in 1985. She convinced generations of women determined to maintain a youthful appearance that her beauty creams were “jars of hope.”

item.title: From 2004: Julia Child, the French Chef for a Jell-O Nation, Dies at 91
item.description: She was a towering figure on the culinary front for more than 40 years, turning the art of French cooking into prime-time television entertainment.
item.dc:creator: Regina Schrambling
item.media:credit: ZUMA Press Wire, via Reuters Connect
item.media:description: Julia Child in 1978. “She demystified French cuisine in a way that had not been done before, in an appealing, straightforward way,” the chef Jacques Pépin said.

item.title: From 1922: Nellie Bly, Journalist, Dies of Pneumonia
item.description: She was best known for her trip around the world in 1889, which she completed in 72 days 6 hours 11 minutes.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: Nellie Bly in 1950. During the course of her career, she not only traveled around the world in less than 80 days, she also went down into the ocean in a diving bell, went up in the air in a balloon and lived in a mental institution as a patient.

item.title: From 2019: Agnès Varda, Influential French New Wave Filmmaker, Dies at 90
item.description: She was closely associated with the film movement known as the New Wave, although her reimagining of cinematic conventions predated it.
item.dc:creator: John Anderson

item.title: Defense Without U.S. Help Is a Live Topic for Canada, Japan and Australia
item.description: The leaders of Japan and Canada are making a unified front on defense cooperation as President Trump raises the pressure over military spending.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Adrian Wyld/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada arriving in Tokyo on Friday as part of a tour that also included Australia.

item.title: Trump Aides Remain Bullish Despite Rising Unemployment and Gas Prices
item.description: The president’s economic advisers insist that any signs of strains are temporary, as they look to sell the president’s agenda entering the midterms.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin Hassett, director of the White House National Economic Council, insisted on Friday that President Trump’s agenda was working — and that any recent turbulence would prove short-lived.

item.title: Democrats Blame Trump For Jobs Report as Republicans Play Defense
item.description: The weaker-than-expected numbers quickly became a source of tension between the two parties ahead of the midterm elections.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Chuck Schumer, Democrat of New York, said the economy could “go over the cliff.”

item.title: Weak Jobs Data Underscores Fed’s Dilemma as War Stokes Inflation Risk
item.description: The Federal Reserve is still widely expected to hold interest rates steady when its officials next meet on March 17-18.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Before Friday’s jobs report, Fed officials had been widely expected to hold interest rates steady again at their next policy meeting in March.

item.title: A Week of Turmoil Leaves Stocks in Negative Territory for 2026
item.description: Market movements this week had already been choppy as investors weighed the inflationary impact of the conflict in the Middle East. On Friday, the jobs report complicated matters.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison

item.title: Iran’s Chokehold on Strait of Hormuz Strains Oil and Gas Shipping
item.description: The threat of prolonged attacks on oil tankers and other vessels is giving Iran an advantage, analysts say.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Ships off the coast of Fujairah, United Arab Emirates, this week.

item.title: Axel Springer Agrees to Buy U.K.’s Telegraph
item.description: The $766 million takeover is the latest twist for The Telegraph, an influential British newspaper whose ownership had been in limbo for years.
item.dc:creator: Michael J. de la Merced
item.media:credit: James Manning/PA Images, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The British newspaper The Daily Telegraph agreed to sell itself to Axel Springer, the European media giant.

item.title: A Vexing Problem for College Students: Course Availability
item.description: A new report looks at course “shutouts,” which can add to the time and cost of getting a degree.
item.dc:creator: Ann Carrns

item.title: Trump’s Strait of Hormuz Problem
item.description: The president campaigned on lowering prices for Americans. That pledge has come under fire this week as the Iran war pushes up the cost of gasoline.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: U.S. gasoline prices have soared since the war on Iran began on Saturday.

item.title: Young and Looking for Work? We Want to Hear from You.
item.description: Unemployment is higher among younger people than other groups, and some companies are cutting back on entry-level hiring. Tell us how you are navigating the start of your career.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember
item.media:description: Job seekers waiting to enter an employment fair in Silver Spring, Md., last year.

item.title: What to know about the jobs report.
item.description: Employers shed 92,000 jobs in February and the unemployment rate rose to 4.4 percent.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember

item.title: European stock indexes fall again, ending a turbulent week for markets.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.media:description: A gas station in Burgos, Spain, on Thursday. A surge in oil and gas prices driven by the Middle East conflict has led to a chaotic week in European stock markets.

item.title: Big Revisions Are a Reason to Question the Jobs Numbers, Not to Dismiss Them
item.description: Economists say estimates from the Bureau of Labor Statistics and other agencies are reliable, but they worry the quality of data is eroding.
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times
item.media:description: Construction of townhouses in Simpsonville, S.C. Home building and other aspects of the economy, in addition to the jobs numbers, have seen large revisions in recent years.

item.title: The Allure of ‘Slop Bowls’ Fades as Consumers Tighten Spending at Restaurants
item.description: Sales and traffic at restaurant chains like Cava, Chipotle and Sweetgreen are falling, as customers grow tired of both salad bowls and their rising price tags.
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell
item.media:credit: Natalie Keyssar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Consumers are growing weary of healthy-but-kinda-mushy meals.

item.title: Health Care Has Become the Lifeblood of the Labor Market
item.description: An aging population is drawing workers to medical and social care, creating reliable jobs and revealing weakness for the rest of the economy.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis
item.media:credit: Morgan Lieberman for The New York Times
item.media:description: The emergency department of a hospital in Los Angeles. As fast as the health care sector has been expanding, it’s unclear whether the supply of workers can keep pace with the ever-growing need.

item.title: Pardon Industry Offers Rich Offenders a Path to Trump
item.description: One inmate paid lobbyists and lawyers with ties to the president’s team and walked free. Others are following his blueprint, but it is not always clear who can deliver.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth P. Vogel
item.media:credit: Bryan Anselm for The New York Times

item.title: U.S. Gas Prices Jump Again as Oil Tops $90 for First Time in Years
item.description: The price of a gallon of gasoline hits its highest level in a year and a half on Friday. Surging oil prices suggest that the increases will continue.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: India Is Turning Back to Russian Oil
item.description: India’s trade deal with President Trump was supposed to end its imports of oil from Russia. But then the conflict in the Middle East has cut off alternative supplies.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A Russian tanker in 2024 navigating the Bosporus, one route used to export oil to buyers abroad.

item.title: Ex-Executive at Blood Filter Start-Up Is Charged With Covering Up Deaths
item.description: ExThera Medical attracted cancer patients to Antigua with the promise that its devices could cure them. Its former chief regulatory officer faces up to three years in prison.
item.dc:creator: John Carreyrou
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times
item.media:description: The clinic in Antigua where cancer patients were treated with ExThera’s blood filter.

item.title: Pokémon Company Objects to White House’s Political Memes
item.description: “No permission was granted for the use of our intellectual property,” the company said. The Trump administration frequently promotes policies with content from video games.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The White House used an image from the new game Pokémon Pokopia in a social media post saying “Make America Great Again.”

item.title: Oil Jumps, Stocks Drop as Investors Grapple With Iran War’s Impact
item.description: Oil futures are at their highest level since July 2024. The S&P 500 is now in negative territory for the year.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner and Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: States Sue to Stop Trump From Reviving Steep Tariffs
item.description: The lawsuit filed by two dozen attorneys general seeks to invalidate the president’s new, 10 percent global tax on imports.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The legal challenge is only the latest twist in a saga that has engulfed President Trump’s economic agenda, which hinges on the use of tariffs.

item.title: McDonald’s C.E.O. Takes a Big Bite Out of a Burger. Maybe Scratch ‘Big.’
item.description: Chris Kempczinski’s rather tentative chomp drew mirth online, even from some competitors.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather

item.title: Savannah Guthrie Plans a Return to ‘Today’
item.description: Ms. Guthrie visited 30 Rockefeller Plaza on Thursday for the first time since her mother, Nancy, went missing in early February.
item.dc:creator: John Koblin
item.media:description: Savannah Guthrie hugs Dylan Dreyer, “Today” meteorologist, during a visit to the set on Thursday.

item.title: How $800 Monthly Car Payments Are Hurting Car Sales
item.description: Cars have become so expensive that many Americans are putting off or not buying new cars, hurting the auto industry.
item.dc:creator: Neal E. Boudette
item.media:description: The average monthly payment for a new car was $774 at the end of last year, and the average new car now costs nearly $50,000.

item.title: Revolut, the British Finance App, Seeks a U.S. Banking Charter
item.description: The company said that the move, if approved, would allow it to offer more products to American customers as it seeks to grow globally.
item.dc:creator: Michael J. de la Merced
item.category: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency
item.media:description: Revolut said that obtaining a U.S. banking license would let it offer Americans products like its own credit cards, as it pursues global growth.

item.title: Iceland Defeats Iceland: A U.K. Supermarket Ends a Trademark Dispute
item.description: Can a company trademark the name of a country? In the case of Iceland, the British grocery store, the answer is no.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.media:description: An Iceland supermarket in Liverpool, England. The grocery chain was founded in 1970.

item.title: Can Anthropic Make Peace With the Pentagon?
item.description: The artificial intelligence start-up has reportedly resumed talks with the Defense Department over use of its tools, with high stakes for both.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dario Amodei, the C.E.O. of Anthropic, may have a new chance to patch up ties with the Pentagon.

item.title: How War in the Persian Gulf Could Spill Into the U.S. Economy
item.description: Rising energy prices, snarled supply chains and higher government debt could all hurt American consumers.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Debris from an intercepted drone started a fire on Wednesday at the Fujairah oil industry zone in the United Arab Emirates.

item.title: ‘No Tax on Overtime’ Rule Isn’t What Some Workers Were Expecting
item.description: President Trump’s simple campaign promise, now reality, includes lots of fine print that is confounding taxpayers and companies this filing season.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.media:credit: Nick Oxford for The New York Times; Lauren Petracca for The New York Times

item.title: Help! The Hotel Had Rodents, and Our Credit Card Won’t Accept a Dispute.
item.description: A couple spent $630 to reserve a room at Yosemite a year in advance, but days before their trip, they learned it had a rodent problem. Why did Citi tell them it was too late?
item.dc:creator: Seth Kugel

item.title: Why Does Child Care Seem Less Affordable Than Ever?
item.description: It has always been expensive, but recently prices have risen faster than inflation.
item.dc:creator: Asmaa Elkeurti and Claire Cain Miller

item.title: They Made It Through the 24 Hours That Rocked South Korea’s Markets
item.description: After explosive growth in recent months, the country’s benchmark stock index plummeted, then rebounded, in the wake of the U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A screen displaying stock prices at the Korea Exchange in Seoul on Wednesday, when stocks plunged.

item.title: They Helped Her Open a Weed Shop. Now They’re Suing for $2.5 Million.
item.description: A dispute between the nonprofit Housing Works and the owner of a cannabis dispensary shows the risks for businesses in an industry that is locked out of traditional financing and resources.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Southall
item.media:credit: Kent J. Edwards for The New York Times
item.media:description: Leeann Mata says that Housing Works has not delivered on the agreement to help develop her cannabis dispensary, Matawana.

item.title: What the Extraordinary Market Volatility in Asia Says About Energy and A.I.
item.description: Stocks in South Korea and Taiwan, the center of global chip making, plunged on fears about energy prices. Their recovery shows the bullishness over artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Currency traders at the Hana Bank headquarters in Seoul on Thursday.

item.title: Top Aide to Labor Secretary Is Placed on Leave
item.description: Several of Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer’s employees are under investigation for official misconduct under her leadership.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Davis O’Brien
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: An aide to Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer was placed on administrative leave Wednesday.

item.title: China Sets Its Lowest Economic Growth Target in Decades
item.description: The goal of between 4.5 percent and 5 percent, announced at a gathering of Communist Party leaders, was the lowest since 1991 and can offer clues about China’s policymaking plans.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Krolik
item.media:description: China’s leader, Xi Jinping, at the Great Hall of the People in Beijing on Wednesday.

item.title: U.S. Court Takes First Steps Toward Ordering Tariff Refunds
item.description: The Trump administration is likely to appeal the move, as it ramps up its attempt to slow or potentially block the repayment of billions in past duties.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm and Ana Swanson
item.media:description: For many importers, the liquidation process is still well underway for millions of goods that were brought ashore during President Trump’s first year back in office.

item.title: Iranian TV and Social Media Project Defiant and Distorted View of the War
item.description: State media and online propagandists are striking a confident posture, despite heavy losses. Some of the content was generated by artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Tiffany Hsu, Steven Lee Myers and Stuart A. Thompson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Travelers Stranded by U.S.-Israel Conflict With Iran Face Endless Flight Cancellations
item.description: Even far from hubs like Dubai and Abu Dhabi, the disruptions from the growing violence have left people jumping “from one cancellation to the next.”
item.dc:creator: Christine Chung, Gabe Castro-Root and Niraj Chokshi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Tourists in Abu Dhabi, in the United Arab Emirates. on Sunday, the day after the United States and Israel struck Iran, setting off a conflict that has caused chaos for travelers.

item.title: What to Know About Iran’s Drones, Which Are Cheap to Launch but Expensive to Intercept
item.description: What to know about Iran’s cheap, noisy Shahed drones and the expensive defense systems trying to stop them.
item.dc:creator: Farah Stockman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
Center for Strategic and International Studies
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jet-powered Shahed drones displayed as part of a military parade in 2024.
